question.text: In which market does the futures pricing model explicitly use TWO interest rates as inputs?

answer.text: Currency derivatives — the Interest Rate Parity model uses both domestic and foreign interest rates to determine the fair futures price
Equity derivatives — the cost-of-carry model uses both risk-free rate and dividend yield
Interest rate derivatives — the bond futures pricing uses both coupon rate and repo rate
All derivative markets use exactly two interest rates for pricing

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Currency futures: F = S × (1 + R_domestic × t) / (1 + R_foreign × t). The formula explicitly uses two interest rates — the domestic currency rate and the foreign currency rate. Equity CoC uses risk-free rate minus dividend yield (not two interest rates). Bond futures use conversion factors and one reference yield. **The Trap:** Equity CoC does use rate minus yield, but the dividend yield is NOT an interest rate. Bond futures pricing involves coupon and market yield but doesn't use the IRP formula. **Cross-Exam Context:** This is a XIII-unique cross-asset comparison testing understanding of different pricing models across Series I, IV, and VIII. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The IRP model explains why currencies with higher interest rates trade at a forward premium — the rate differential is priced into futures.
